text:
WHOLE BODY 18F-FDG PET/CT SCAN

EXAMINATION: PET/CT whole body with 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (18F-FDG), acquired from vertex to mid-thigh with dedicated brain and skull base imaging

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with newly diagnosed invasive cutaneous melanoma of the left posterior shoulder (Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V). Known elevated LDH at 387 U/L. Referred for initial systemic staging prior to treatment planning. BRAF V600E mutation confirmed on molecular testing.

TECHNIQUE: The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to tracer administration. Blood glucose at time of injection was 94 mg/dL. The patient received 12.4 mCi of 18F-FDG intravenously. Whole-body image acquisition commenced 62 minutes post-injection. Low-dose CT attenuation correction was performed. Dedicated brain and skull base sequences were included. Images were reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes with maximum intensity projection (MIP) reconstructions. Fused PET/CT images were reviewed in all planes.

COMPARISON: No prior imaging available for comparison.

---

FINDINGS:

PRIMARY LESION:
There is an intensely hypermetabolic focus in the soft tissues of the left posterior shoulder, measuring approximately 1.8 cm in cross-sectional diameter on the CT component, with a markedly elevated FDG avidity (SUVmax 8.2). The lesion appears to involve the dermal and subdermal layers without evidence of underlying osseous involvement. There is no invasion of the adjacent scapular musculature. The morphology and metabolic activity are highly consistent with the known primary melanoma at this site.

LYMPH NODES:
Bilateral axillary regions demonstrate pathologically enlarged and intensely FDG-avid lymph nodes.
- Right axilla: A dominant lymph node measuring 2.1 cm in short axis with markedly elevated tracer uptake (SUVmax 11.4). Several additional smaller right axillary nodes demonstrate borderline or mildly elevated FDG activity, the largest of which measures approximately 0.9 cm and may represent early nodal involvement versus reactive change; this is favored to represent metastatic involvement given the degree of tracer activity and the setting of known high-grade melanoma.
- Left axilla: A dominant node measuring 1.8 cm in short axis with elevated FDG uptake (SUVmax 9.7). A cluster of additional sub-centimeter nodes in the left axilla demonstrate mildly elevated FDG activity.
- No supraclavicular, internal mammary, mediastinal, hilar, retroperitoneal, iliac, or inguinal lymph nodes demonstrate abnormal morphology or FDG avidity beyond expected physiologic distribution.

SUBCUTANEOUS SOFT TISSUE:
Multiple hypermetabolic subcutaneous soft tissue nodules are identified.
- Right flank: The most prominent subcutaneous nodule measures approximately 1.4 cm in greatest dimension with an SUVmax of 6.8. Two additional smaller foci of FDG uptake are noted in the adjacent right flank subcutaneous tissue, measuring approximately 0.7 cm and 0.5 cm, each with mild-to-moderate tracer avidity (SUVmax range 3.8-4.2), consistent with additional in-transit/subcutaneous melanoma deposits.
- Left axillary/chest wall soft tissues: Several small foci of FDG uptake are noted in the subcutaneous tissue along the left lateral chest wall and inferior left axilla, individually measuring less than 1.0 cm, with SUVmax values in the range of 3.5-4.9. These are morphologically and metabolically consistent with additional subcutaneous satellite/in-transit deposits.
- No FDG-avid subcutaneous lesions identified elsewhere on the torso, extremities, or head and neck.

LIVER:
The liver is mildly enlarged, measuring approximately 17.8 cm in craniocaudal span. Four discrete hepatic lesions with FDG avidity are identified, distributed in both lobes:
- Right lobe, posterior segment (segment VI/VII): The largest hepatic lesion measures 3.1 cm with a markedly elevated SUVmax of 14.2. The lesion appears hypodense on the low-dose CT component with a somewhat irregular internal architecture. There is no adjacent biliary ductal dilatation or capsular retraction.
- Right lobe, anterior segment (segment V/VIII): A second lesion measuring 1.8 cm with FDG avidity of SUVmax 10.1. Morphologically similar to the above.
- Left lobe (segment III/IVa junction): A third lesion measuring 1.2 cm with SUVmax 7.9.
- Left lobe (segment II): A fourth, smaller lesion measuring 0.9 cm with SUVmax 6.3, the most subtle of the hepatic deposits but clearly above hepatic background activity.
No biliary obstruction is identified. The portal and hepatic veins are patent on CT. The gallbladder is unremarkable. No perihepatic ascites.

LUNGS AND PLEURA:
The lungs are well aerated without pleural effusion or pneumothorax. No parenchymal nodules, masses, or consolidation with pathologic FDG avidity are identified. Specifically, no pulmonary metastases are detected. Mediastinal and hilar structures are unremarkable. The visualized central airways are patent.

BRAIN AND SKULL:
Dedicated brain images demonstrate normal physiologic cortical FDG distribution without focal areas of abnormal hypermetabolism or hypometabolism to suggest intracranial metastatic disease. No leptomeningeal or dural-based FDG-avid lesions are identified. Skull base is unremarkable.

BONE MARROW / SKELETON:
The axial and appendicular skeleton demonstrates normal physiologic marrow FDG distribution throughout, without focal areas of increased tracer uptake to suggest osseous metastatic involvement. Specifically, the spine, pelvis, ribs, sternum, and visualized long bones are unremarkable. No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified on the CT component.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal FDG avidity above background is identified.

SPLEEN:
Mild diffuse splenic FDG uptake consistent with physiologic activity. The spleen is borderline enlarged at 13.1 cm in craniocaudal dimension; this may reflect reactive splenomegaly in the setting of metastatic disease. No discrete focal splenic lesions.

KIDNEYS AND RETROPERITONEUM:
Bilateral kidneys demonstrate normal size, morphology, and physiologic tracer excretion. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy with pathologic FDG avidity. No pelvic lymph nodes or pelvic masses are identified.

THYROID:
The thyroid gland is homogeneous in echotexture without focal FDG-avid nodules. Mildly increased diffuse thyroid FDG uptake noted; clinical correlation with thyroid function testing recommended given patient's known history of hypothyroidism.

OTHER:
No abnormal FDG uptake in the peritoneum, omentum, or gastrointestinal tract beyond physiologic distribution. No ascites. Visualized bowel loops are unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. INTENSELY HYPERMETABOLIC PRIMARY MELANOMA, LEFT POSTERIOR SHOULDER. The primary lesion demonstrates high-grade metabolic activity (SUVmax 8.2) consistent with the known diagnosis of invasive superficial spreading melanoma with adverse histologic features.

2. BILATERAL AXILLARY NODAL METASTASES. Bilateral hypermetabolic axillary lymphadenopathy (right axilla dominant node 2.1 cm, SUVmax 11.4; left axilla dominant node 1.8 cm, SUVmax 9.7) confirming regional lymph node involvement bilaterally. Additional sub-centimeter bilateral axillary nodes with borderline-to-mild FDG avidity may represent additional sites of early nodal metastatic disease.

3. MULTIPLE HYPERMETABOLIC SUBCUTANEOUS NODULES. Several in-transit and subcutaneous melanoma deposits in the right flank (dominant lesion 1.4 cm, SUVmax 6.8) and along the left axilla/lateral chest wall, consistent with in-transit metastatic disease.

4. HEPATIC METASTASES, FOUR LESIONS BILATERAL. FDG-avid hepatic metastases in both lobes of the liver, ranging from 0.9 to 3.1 cm, with the largest demonstrating markedly elevated FDG avidity (SUVmax 14.2), consistent with active metastatic disease. Findings qualify as M1c disease.

5. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY, OSSEOUS, CEREBRAL, OR OTHER DISTANT ORGAN METASTASES at this time.

OVERALL IMPRESSION: Extensive hypermetabolic disease burden consistent with Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma involving the primary site, bilateral regional lymph nodes, multiple subcutaneous in-transit deposits, and hepatic parenchyma. The absence of pulmonary, skeletal, and intracranial involvement is noted. Findings are concordant with clinical and pathologic staging. Correlation with LDH, serum protein, and molecular profiling results is recommended for prognostic stratification and treatment planning.

Interpreting Radiologist: [Electronically signed]
Nuclear Medicine and Radiology
Report Status: FINAL
